title: Cat Yellow Mucus from Both Nostrils: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Find out why your cat has yellow nasal discharge, when to call a vet, and why a photo of the mucus is essential for a fast diagnosis.
content_html:
What is Yellow Nasal Discharge in Cats?

Yellow mucus, often referred to as purulent discharge, is usually a sign of an active inflammatory or infectious process. While clear discharge can be caused by simple allergies or dust, yellow or green discharge typically indicates that white blood cells are responding to an infection. In cats, this is frequently associated with Feline Upper Respiratory Infection (URI) complexes, which can involve viruses like Feline Herpesvirus or Calicivirus, often complicated by secondary bacterial infections.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for yellow nasal discharge from both nostrils is Medium. While it may not be an immediate life-threatening crisis, it indicates an infection that requires veterinary attention, typically within 24 to 48 hours. Cats are obligatory nasal breathers and rely on their sense of smell to eat. If the congestion becomes severe, your cat may stop eating or drinking, leading to dehydration. Seek emergency care immediately if your cat is breathing with an open mouth, acting extremely lethargic, or has completely stopped eating.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear photo of your cat’s nose before cleaning it can be a vital tool for your veterinarian. Mucus can vary in consistency and volume throughout the day, and your cat may sneeze it away or groom it off before you arrive at the clinic. A photo provides the vet with a snapshot of the peak severity, helping them determine the type of infection and the level of urgency required for treatment. It also serves as a baseline to track if the symptoms are improving after medication is started.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human cold medicine for nasal discharge?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give your cat over-the-counter human medications as many are highly toxic to felines. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Is yellow nasal discharge contagious to my other cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, many respiratory infections in cats are highly contagious. It is recommended to isolate the sick cat and wash your hands between handling pets until a vet confirms the cause.

faqs.question: How can I help my cat breathe better at home?
faqs.answer: You can use a soft, warm, damp cloth to gently wipe away crusty discharge. Additionally, placing your cat in a steamy bathroom for 10-15 minutes can help loosen the mucus and provide temporary relief.
